coredata.dc:title: Enhancement of corrosion resistance of structural timber by injecting boron preservatives labeled with fluorescent carbon dots into living trees
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Liang
Su, Hang
Wang, Xuan
Zhang, Yaoli
Sun, Shijing
Cai, Liping
coredata.dc:description: The preservation of wood from industrial crops is critical for enhancing its durability and extending its utility in structural applications. Wood preservation treatment in standing trees, also known as injection treatment of living trees, involves injecting a wood preservation solution directly into the tree. This method offers advantages of easy operation and deep penetration. To improve the treatment efficiency of injecting wood preservative solution into trees, it is important to monitor the flow of the preservative solution during the treatment process. This allows for real-time adjustments and ensures optimal distribution of the preservative throughout the wood. The use of the fluorescence effect of carbon dots to investigate the rise of wood preservative solutions during the wood preservation treatment in standing trees has not been previously reported. Therefore, yellow, fluorescent carbon dots labeled boron wood preservatives (BX/YCDs/SiO2) were prepared to study the rising patterns of wood preservative solutions. After injection treatment, fluorescent BX/YCDs/SiO2 could be found from the injection port to the treetops under a fluorescence microscope, showing different fluorescence colors. Compared to traditional wood preservative treatments, injecting BX/YCDs/SiO2 into living trees significantly improved the decay resistance of the treated timber and reduced preservative leaching rate. Additionally, the mechanical properties of standing trees injected BX/YCDs//SiO2 increased significantly compared to standing trees treated with boron preservatives by immersion. These findings provide an effective solution for wood preservation, aligning with the need for resource-efficient treatment methods for industrial forestry products. This work demonstrate the potential of BX/YCDs/SiO2-treated standing trees to improve the performance of structural timber, offering a sustainable and efficient approach for wood preservation in industrial applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Wood preservation
Injection treatment
Distribution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurements of BX/YCDs/SiO2 in live trees
Marking of BX/YCDs/SiO2 preservatives in the height direction of living trees
Marking of BX/YCDs/SiO2 diameter direction in living trees
Decay resistance of poplar injected BX/YCDs/SiO2
Mechanical properties of poplar injected BX/YCDs/SiO2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of preparation of fluorescent wood preservatives BX/YCDs/SiO2.
Schematic diagram of injection treatment with BX/YCDs/SiO2.
Fluorescence spectra of YCDs and BX/YCDs/SiO2. (a) Emission spectra of YCDs at different excitation wavelengths; (b) Optimal excitation peak and emission peak of BX/YCDs/SiO2.
TEM images of BX/YCDs/SiO2 before and after the reaction. (a) unreacted borax; (b) unreacted YCDs; (c) BX/YCDs after the reaction; (d) BX/YCDs/SiO2 after reaction; (e) BX/YCDs/SiO2 at 200 nm scale.
Infrared spectra of BX/YCDs and BX/YCDs/SiO2 before and after the reaction. (a) BX/YCDs; (b) BX/YCDs/SiO2.
Fluorescence micrographs of untreated wood and poplar samples at different heights treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 after injection treatment: (a) untreated wood; (b) T0; (c) T3; (d) T6; (e) T9; (f) T12.
Fluorescence spectra of untreated wood and poplar samples at different heights treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 after injection treatment.
Electron microscopic energy spectra of boron in poplar samples at different heights treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 after injection treatment.
The distribution of BX/YCDs/SiO2 preservatives on cross sections of poplar trees at different heights.

Fluorescence micrographs of untreated wood and poplar samples on the cross section of T0 treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 after injection treatment: (a) untreated wood; (b)injection port; (c) middle position; (d) far from injection port.
Fluorescence spectra of different cross section position of poplar samples at T0 treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 after injection treatment.
FITR spectra of untreated wood and different cross-sectional position of poplar samples at T0 treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 after injection treatment.

XPS spectra of poplar samples and untreated wood at different cross-sectional positions after BX/YCDs/SiO2 fluorescent wood preservative treatment; (a) injection port; (b) the middle position; (c) far from the injection port; (d) untreated wood.
The mass loss rate of the cross sections at different height of samples treated by injection treatment with BX/YCDs/SiO2.
The leaching rate of cross-sectional samples at T0 by different treatment.

(a)Flexural strength and compression strength of the poplar wood treated with different antiseptic treatments, (b) Flexural MOE and compression MOE of the poplar wood treated with different antiseptic treatments.

The contents of different carbon atomic structures in poplar wood samples at different cross-sectional positions in XPS of untreated wood and wood treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 fluorescent wood preservative.

head.ce:title: Enhancement of corrosion resistance of structural timber by injecting boron preservatives labeled with fluorescent carbon dots into living trees

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preservation of wood from industrial crops is critical for enhancing its durability and extending its utility in structural applications. Wood preservation treatment in standing trees, also known as injection treatment of living trees, involves injecting a wood preservation solution directly into the tree. This method offers advantages of easy operation and deep penetration. To improve the treatment efficiency of injecting wood preservative solution into trees, it is important to monitor the flow of the preservative solution during the treatment process. This allows for real-time adjustments and ensures optimal distribution of the preservative throughout the wood. The use of the fluorescence effect of carbon dots to investigate the rise of wood preservative solutions during the wood preservation treatment in standing trees has not been previously reported. Therefore, yellow, fluorescent carbon dots labeled boron wood preservatives (BX/YCDs/SiO2) were prepared to study the rising patterns of wood preservative solutions. After injection treatment, fluorescent BX/YCDs/SiO2 could be found from the injection port to the treetops under a fluorescence microscope, showing different fluorescence colors. Compared to traditional wood preservative treatments, injecting BX/YCDs/SiO2 into living trees significantly improved the decay resistance of the treated timber and reduced preservative leaching rate. Additionally, the mechanical properties of standing trees injected BX/YCDs//SiO2 increased significantly compared to standing trees treated with boron preservatives by immersion. These findings provide an effective solution for wood preservation, aligning with the need for resource-efficient treatment methods for industrial forestry products. This work demonstrate the potential of BX/YCDs/SiO2-treated standing trees to improve the performance of structural timber, offering a sustainable and efficient approach for wood preservation in industrial applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Wood preservation for construction timber focuses on improving its durability and longevity by protecting it from various forms of degradation, including decay, rot, insect damage (such as termites and wood-boring beetles), weathering effects, and mold as well as mildew growth (Peng et al., 2023; Shukla and Kamdem, 2012). To improve the treatment efficiency of injecting wood preservative solution into trees, it is important to monitor the flow of the preservative solution during the treatment process. This enables real-time adjustments, ensuring optimal distribution of the preservative throughout the wood. It helps preserve the structural integrity and appearance of timber in buildings and other structures, especially in harsh or variable environmental conditions. Wood preservation treatment in standing trees (injection treatment to living trees) is a method by injecting wood preservation solution from the bottom of the trunk and utilizing the transpiration of trees to deliver it upwards to the crown, which has the advantages of easy operation and deep penetration (Fan et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2019). Wood treated through injection method demonstrates excellent decay resistance and reduced preservative loss (Zhao et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2022). Additionally, injection treatment has a minimal impact on the mechanical properties of wood (Wen et al., 2023a), making it a promising technique with broad development potential. To study the rising patterns and penetration of wood preservatives solutions, attempts have been made to mix dye solutions with preservative solutions. However, the properties and structures of dye molecules differ from those of preservative molecules. Under the influence of transpiration, their retention time in the xylem vary, causing them to reach different parts of the tree. As a result, they cannot accurately trace the flow of the preservative solution, behaving similarly to the separation process in chromatography. To enhance the treatment efficiency of injecting wood preservative solution into trees, monitoring the flow of the preservative solution during the treatment process is crucial. This enables real-time adjustments and ensures the preservative is optimally distributed throughout the wood.

Carbon dots, as one of novel fluorescent nanomaterials, are widely used in the field of biology due to their excellent photostability and chemical stability, high biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity (Xiao et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2011; Choi et al., 2017; Gao et al., 2021). Lin et al (Lin et al., 2017). prepared carbon quantum dots by high-temperature pyrolysis of citric acid and coupled them with tryptophan to obtain indole-3-acetic acid (IPA) modified carbon quantum dots (IPA-CDs). IPA-CDs was used for further tracking the signal transduction process of IPA, shedding light on its functional mechanism of IPA in crop production. Therefore, it is feasible to use carbon dots for fluorescent labeling of wood preservatives to explore the rising of wood preservative solutions. However, the emission peak range of lignin spontaneous fluorescence is between 380nm and 480nm. To avoid interference from lignin on the fluorescence imaging of carbon dots, the selection of carbon dots is crucial. Different carbon sources and preparation methods can produce CDs with different excitation and emission wavelengths, which can exhibit fluorescence of different colors (Park et al., 2023; Pan et al., 2012). The fluorescence intensity of CDs also varies significantly with different heating temperatures, heating times, and solution acidity and alkalinity (Yang et al., 2019; Duan et al., 2023). Hao et al (Hao et al., 2023). prepared carbon quantum dots with bright yellow fluorescence and a quantum yield of 17.2 % using ortho-phenylenediamine as the precursor and glycerol as the solvent. Li et al. (2023) studied the redshift mechanism of full-color CDs by selecting citric acid and Nile blue as raw materials with complex composition structures. Four types of fluorescent carbon quantum dots, blue, green, yellow, and red, were prepared by changing the reaction solvent, with average particle sizes ranging from 2.7 to 3.2 nm.

Borates, as a broad-spectrum wood preservative, have good water solubility and are suitable for wood preservation treatment of standing timber. Many studies have explored the synthesis of composites of carbon dots and boron compounds through hydrothermal reactions, but most of them have focused on the surface modification or passivation of carbon dots (Chen et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2022; Fu et al., 2024). The abundant silicon hydroxyl groups on the surface of nano silica give it a large specific surface area and surface polarity, which can be used for surface passivation of carbon quantum dots. Moreover, silica is non-toxic, biocompatible (Karimi et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2015), and can improve the poor anti-loss performance after borate wood preservation treatment.

In this experiment, fluorescent carbon quantum dots were synthesized using the solvothermal method, with ortho-phenylenediamine as the carbon source and glycerol as the solvent. Fluorescent wood preservatives were then prepared by covalently bonding borax with CDs. The composite structure of the fluorescent wood preservatives was analyzed using the transmission electron microscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and other testing methods. Fast-growing poplar trees were treated with injection treatment using fluorescent wood preservation solution. The upward trend of the wood preservation solution was explored through fluorescent labeling. At the same time, the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy were used to explain the upward and lateral penetration of the wood preservation solution in live standing poplar trees. Compared to traditional wood preservative treatments, this study examined the decay resistance, mechanical properties and preservative leaching rate of the treated timber. The findings provide valuable insights for enhancing the corrosion resistance of structural timber, thereby extending its application in building and construction projects.

The fast-growing poplar (Populus euramevicana cv.I-107) purchased in Suqian, Jiangsu Province was planted in the experimental field of Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, China (East 118.48°, North 32°).

0.4 g of ortho-phenylenediamine was dissolved thoroughly in 8 ml of glycerol, and then was poured into a reaction vessel at 220 ℃ in an oil bath for 1 h. Subsequently, the reaction product was dissolved in deionized water, and then was filtered to eliminate large-size particles. The filtrate was subjected to dialysis in distilled water for 24 h, and subsequently freeze-dried to produce YCDs. An appropriate amount of borax and YCDs was dissolved in deionized water, transferred to a reaction vessel, and reacted in an oil bath. The ratio of borax to CDs was 5:1, the reaction temperature was 220 ℃, and the reaction time was 6 h The reaction solutions were respectively configured into 0.5 wt% fluorescent borax solution per kilogram. An appropriate amount of ammonia was added and stirred well. With a clean separating funnel, tetraethyl orthosilicate was dropped dropwise into the solution and stirred continuously to obtain BX/YCDs/SiO2 ( Fig. 1).

The fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to test the excitation and emission spectra of YCDs and their fluorescently labeled preservative aqueous solutions. The LS-55 from USA fluorescence spectrometer made in the USA was used for fluorescence analysis. The excitation and emission wavelengths ranged from 200 nm to 800 nm, with an interval of 10 nm for measurement.

A transmission electron microscope (TEM), the model of JEM-1400 made in Japan, was utilized to examine the morphological structure of BX/YCDs/SiO2 before and after the synthesis. The TEM operated at an acceleration voltage of 20 kV. The diluted solution was applied to a copper grid and dried under a baking lamp. Following image capture, the diameters of BX, YCDs, BX/YCDs, and BX/YCDs/SiO₂ were calculated based on the images using the software of Image.

FTIR analysis was conducted on the prepared BX/YCDs and BX/YCDs/SiO₂ to verify the binding of carbon dots to BX. The VERTEX 80 V spectrometer (made in Germany) was employed for this purpose. To prepare for testing, the dried samples were ground into powder, combined with KBr, and compressed into a disk. Scans were performed across a wavelength range of 400 cm⁻¹ to 4000 cm⁻¹ , with two scans completed.

Poplar trees were divided into two groups, with three trees in each group. One group received no treatment, while the other group was injected with BX/YCDs/SiO2 solutions. At 30 cm from the ground on each poplar tree, a 10 ml syringe needle was used to evenly drill 2 holes around the sapling, with a depth of 1–2 times the width of the tree rings. 30 ml of borax composite solution was injected into each well daily for one week. After logging, every 30 cm trunk was cut from the injection port, and the injection port was marked as T0, and the rest was marked as T3, T6, T9, T12 in sequence ( Fig. 2). Samples were taken from the injection port positions, middle positions, and positions far away from the injection port at different heights of cross-sections.

Untreated samples were placed in a vacuum chamber at 0.1 MPa for 30 min. A solution of 0.5 wt% boric acid/borax (BA/BX) was then added and held for 3 d. After releasing the pressure, the samples were removed and the filter paper was used to clean their surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of BX/YCDs/SiO2 in live trees
ce:section.ce:para: An automatic slide slicer was used to slice the cross-sections of poplar wood at different height positions after wood preservation treatment with yellow fluorescent labeled wood preservatives. The cross-section slices were obtained with a thickness of 20 μm. The fluorescence performance of these slices was observed using the BX51 fluorescence microscope.

The FluoroMax-4 instantaneous fluorescence spectrometer was used to test the excitation spectra of poplar wood powder at different heights and cross-sectional positions after wood preservation treatment with different fluorescent labeled wood preservation solutions. The excitation and emission light ranged from 200 nm to 800 nm, with an interval of 10 nm for testing.

The slices of T0-T12 were placed to be observed on the sample stage with conductive adhesive and use a vacuum coating machine to spray gold to increase the conductivity of the sample. Then, the poplar samples at different heights, following wood preservation treatment, were observed using an environmental scanning electron microscope (SEM), Quanta 2000, with an acceleration voltage of 20kv. Subsequently, the boron element in the observed area was measured using an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) plugin.

The processed poplar samples from different parts were cut into small wooden strips and then ground using a grinder to obtain 80-mesh wood powder. The powder was dried in an oven at 103 ℃. The AXIS UltraDLD X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the chemical element content on the surface of poplar treated with standing tree.

The wood decay resistance test followed the GB/T 13942.1–2009 standard (GB/T 13942.1–2009 standard, 2009), using samples with dimensions of 20 × 20 × 20 mm. After 12 weeks of infection with white rot fungus, the mycelium and impurities on the surface of the injected poplar samples were gently scraped off and oven-dried at 103 ± 2 °C. The mass of each sample before and after the experiment were determined, and the rate of mass-loss was calculated using Eq. 1.:
(1) η= (W1 -W2) / W1 × 100%
where η is the rate of mass-loss (%), W1 is the total dry mass before measurement (g), W2 iis the dry mass after measurement (g).

The leaching rate test followed the GB/T 29905–2013 standard (GB/T 29905–2013 standard, 2013), using samples with dimensions of 20 × 20 × 20 mm. The injected poplar samples and untreated poplar samples were weighed to calculate the mass of the sample absorbing the solution during the injection treatment. The sample was placed in a 500 ml beaker and immersed in 180 ml of deionized water for stirring. To prevent moisture evaporation, the beaker was sealed with plastic film. Deionized water was replaced at 6, 24, and 48 h initially, then every 48 h for a period totaling 14 days. The filtrate from leaching rate test was harvested and an atomic absorption spectrometer was used to determine the main element content in the solution. The leaching rate was then calculated using the following formula:
(2) L= (18 × c1) / (1000 × mG × C) × 100%
where c1 is the concentration of major elements in the filtrate (mg/L), C is the mass fraction of the component to be examined in the wood preservative solution (%), mG is the mass of the sample absorbing the solution during the injection treatment (g).

The flexural strength test followed the GB/T 1927.10–2021 standard (GB/T, 1927.10–2021 standard, 2021), using sample dimensions of 150 × 10 × 10 mm. The linear flexural strength with the moisture content W was calculated using Eq. 3:
(3) σw= (3 ×Pmax × l) / (2 × b × h × h)
Where
σ
W
is the flexural strength (MPa), Pmax is the damage load (N), W is the samples moisture content (%), l is the span between two supports (mm), b is the width of samples (mm), h is the height of samples (mm).

The compression strength test followed the GB/T 1927.11–2021 standard (GB/T, 1927.11–2021 standard, 2021) using sample dimensions of 20 × 20 × 20 mm. The linear flexural strength with the moisture content W was calculated using Eq. 4:
(4) σw=Pmax /b / t
where
σ
W
is the compression strength (MPa), Pmax is the damage load (N), W is the samples moisture content (%), b is the width of samples (mm), t is the thickness of samples (mm).

To investigate the performance of the fluorescent labeled wood preservatives BX/YCDs/SiO2, the fluorescence spectra of YCDs and BX/YCDs/SiO2 aqueous solutions was analyzed at different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 3, under the condition of excitation slit and emission slit of 10 nm, the YCDs aqueous solution was exposed to excitation light with wavelengths ranging from 380 nm to 440 nm. At an excitation wavelength of 420 nm, YCDs displayed a maximum fluorescence emission peak at 570 nm. The excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of the BX/YCDs/SiO2 aqueous solution after the reaction were the same as those of the YCDs aqueous solution. YCDs aqueous solution and BX/YCDs/SiO2 aqueous solution exhibited yellow fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light irradiation, respectively.

The morphological changes of BX/YCDs/SiO2 before and after the reaction were observed by the TEM. Fig. 4 shows TEM images of BX/YCDs/SiO2 before and after the reaction. Fig. 4a and 4b show the unreacted borax and YCDs distributed in the solution. Borax was a light-colored short rod-shaped crystal with a length of 72.49 ± 9.48 nm, while YCDs were dark-colored nanoparticles with a diameter size distribution of 3.18 ± 1.23 nm. Fig. 4c displays BX/YCDs synthesized by reaction, with dark short rod-shaped crystals, explaining the combination of borax and YCDs. Fig. 4d displays the BX/YCDs/ SiO2 synthesized by reaction, which was uniformly spherical and the particles were well separated from each other. The diameter distributions of BX/YCDs/SiO2 synthesized with different sizes were 487.34 ± 105.61 nm, respectively. From Fig. 4e, the enlarged internal morphology structure of BX/YCDs/ SiO2 can be seen, with multiple dark BX/YCDs wrapped inside the light-colored spherical object. The nano SiO2 particles generated by the reaction between tetraethyl silicate and ammonia water were well encapsulated around BX/YCDs.

In Fig. 5a, the water molecules in borax exhibited a characteristic absorption peak of O-H stretching vibration at 3218 cm−1 (Zhang et al., 2010). Borax exhibited a characteristic absorption peak of triangular BO3 3- asymmetric stretching vibration at 1448 cm−1 and a characteristic absorption peak of tetrahedral BO4 5- asymmetric stretching vibration at 1193 cm−1 (Cui et al., 2011). The nano SiO2 formed by tetraethyl silicate exhibited characteristic absorption peaks of Si-O flexural vibration at 468 cm−1, Si-O stretching vibration at 796 cm−1, and Si-O-Si antisymmetric stretching vibration at 1095 cm−1 (Chu and Jiang, 2020). YCDs exhibited characteristic absorption peaks of O-H stretching vibration at 3415 cm−1 and CO stretching vibration at 1630 cm−1 (Yang et al., 2012). Compared with borax, the characteristic absorption peaks of O-H stretching vibration and tetrahedral BO4 5-asymmetric stretching vibration of BX/YCDs disappeared, while the absorption of CO stretching vibration occurred. It can be inferred that the - OH of borax reacted with the - COOH of CDs, causing YCDs to bind to the surface of borax crystals, which was consistent with Fig. 4c. In Fig. 5b, compared with nano SiO2, the absorption bands of BX/YCDs/SiO2 were basically the same, but the peak areas were different, indicating that nano SiO2 completely wraps BX/YCDs inside, which was consistent with Fig. 4e.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Marking of BX/YCDs/SiO2 preservatives in the height direction of living trees
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the good distribution of BX/YCDs/SiO2 in living poplar after the injection treatment, analysis was conducted on poplar trees at different height positions after wood preservation treatment with BX/YCDs/SiO2. Fig. 6 displays the fluorescence micrographs of untreated wood and poplar wood at different height positions after the injection treatment with BX/YCDs/SiO2. All slices of the samples were taken under the same exposure conditions. As shown in Fig. 6a, there was a significant fluorescence reaction in the intercellular layer of the untreated material, which appeared as bright blue fluorescence. This was due to the highest concentration of lignin in the intercellular layer, which belonged to the phenomenon of spontaneous fluorescence of lignin (Donason and Radotic, 2013). The transport tissue of broad-leaved wood is usually the xylem, and the diameter of the vessels in the cells inside the xylem is the largest and the end walls at the upper and lower ends are not closed (Wen et al., 2023b). Poplar has well-developed and mainly complex vessels (Chen et al., 2012), so the preservative solutions mainly flow through the vessels in the xylem when it is transported upwards through transpiration in the poplar tree. Due to the nanoscale size of BX/YCDs/SiO2, it penetrated well into wood cells. Compared with untreated materials, fluorescence reactions of different colors were observed at T0-T12. During the ascent of fluorescent preservative solutions in tree trunks through transpiration, the time it took for the solution to reach different heights varied (Denny, 2012), the degree of penetration was different, and the reaction time with wood vessels, fibers, and ray cell walls was different. The degree of yellow fluorescence superimposed on wood vessels, fibers, and ray cell walls also varied. The fluorescence intensity of the wood vessels, fibers, and ray cell walls from T0 to T3 was strong, showing yellow-green fluorescence. As the height of poplar trees increased, the content of BX/YCDs/SiO2 solution distribution in the cross section decreased. The fluorescence intensity of the vessels, wood fibers, and wood ray cell walls significantly decreased from T6 to T9, showing dark green fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity of vessels, wood fiber, and wood ray cell wall at T12 continued to decrease, showing blue-green fluorescence.

To verify the results of fluorescence microscopy analysis, fluorescence spectroscopy tests were conducted on untreated wood and wood at different height positions treated after the injection treatment. As shown in Fig. 7, when the excitation wavelength was 420 nm, samples of poplar at different heights after injection treatment with BX/YCDs/SiO2 exhibited maximum fluorescence intensity excitation peaks at different wavelengths. At T0 and T3, maximum fluorescence peaks were occurred at 557 nm and 553 nm, respectively. For T6 and T9, the peaks with highest fluorescence intensity were observed at 530 nm and 525 nm, respectively. At T12, the excitation peak with the maximum fluorescence intensity was generated at 508 nm. Compared with the single BX/YCDs/SiO2 fluorescence emission peak, with the increase of tree height, the fluorescence emission peak of poplar treated with wood preservation treatment showed a blue shift phenomenon, which may be due to the interaction between the components on the poplar cell wall and BX/YCDs/SiO2, changing the molecular energy levels of BX/YCDs/SiO2 in poplar samples at different heights after the treatment and altering their emission wavelength (Khrebtov et al., 2018). The wood fluorescence intensity of the sample at T12 was the lowest, while the wood fluorescence intensity of the sample at T0 was the highest. As the height of poplar trees increased, the fluorescence intensity of poplar trees at various positions gradually decreased, indicating a gradual decrease in the content of BX/YCDs/SiO2.

Due to the nanoscale size of BX/YCDs/SiO2, its distribution cannot be shown in Fig. 6. Boron element mapping images were obtained by the SEM and EDS to confirm the content distribution of BX/YCDs/SiO2 on cross-sections at different heights after the injection treatment. The higher the content of the measured elements, the denser the element points. As shown in Fig. 8, BX/YCDs/SiO2 was mainly distributed on the cell walls of vessels and wood fibers. The boron content of poplar wood treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 after the injection treatment was higher from T0 to T3 and decreased from T6 to T12. As the height and position of poplar trees increased, the content of boron element gradually decreased, which was consistent with the results of fluorescence microscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Marking of BX/YCDs/SiO2 diameter direction in living trees
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence reaction of BX/YCDs/SiO2 standing trees after the wood preservation treatment at different height positions was studied using the fluorescence reaction of YCDs to analyze the upward trend of wood preservation solution in the xylem. By observing and taking fluorescence microscopy images of cross-sections at different heights of the trees treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 wood preservation, the distribution of BX/YCDs/SiO2 in the diameter direction at different heights of poplar trees treated after the injection treatment was analyzed. As shown in Fig. 9, T0 is near the injection port. When BX/YCDs/SiO2 rose with the sap and reached T3-T12, the distribution area of the medicine decreased away from the injection port. As for samples with the same injection concentration, the BX/YCDs/SiO2 distribution area of T0 was the largest. As the height of poplar trees increased, the distribution of preservatives in the cross-section gradually decreased.

To investigate the interaction between BX/YCDs/SiO2 and poplar cell walls, fluorescence micrographs of wood samples treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 preservative solution at different cross-sectional positions and untreated wood were studied. All slices of the samples were taken under the same exposure conditions. As Fig. 10 displayed, there was a significant fluorescence reaction in the intercellular layer of the untreated wood, which appeared as bright blue fluorescence. Compared with untreated wood, different fluorescence colors were observed at the injection port position, middle position, and away from the injection port position. The pits on the cell wall were the main channels for water and nutrient exchange between adjacent cells, so preservative solutions could be transported through the pits on the wood fibers. The lateral flow of preservative solutions in poplar was dependent on the wood rays and intercellular permeability (He et al., 2014). During the transverse penetration process of the yellow fluorescent marked wood preservation solution through the pits between the vessels and the wood fibers, as well as between the vessels and the wood rays, the time for the fluorescent wood preservation solution to reach different positions from the injection port was different, and the reaction time durations with other wood vessels, fibers, and ray cell walls were also different. The degrees of yellow fluorescence superimposed on the vessel and wood fiber cell walls were also different. The concentration of BX/YCDs/SiO2 solution near the injection port was high, and the yellow fluorescence of BX/YCDs/SiO2 solution overlapped with the blue fluorescence of lignin. The vessel and wood fiber cell walls at the injection port exhibited yellow green fluorescence. As the distance from the injection port gradually increased, the concentration of BX/YCDs/SiO2 solution fixed in the cross-section decreased. The middle position of the conduit and the wood fiber cell wall exhibited light green fluorescence with reduced fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity of the vessels and wood fiber cell walls located far away from the injection port significantly decreased, showing blue-green fluorescence.

To verify the results of fluorescence microscopy analysis, fluorescence spectroscopy tests were conducted on untreated wood and poplar samples at different cross-sectional positions after the wood preservation treatment. As shown in Fig. 11, when the excitation wavelength was 420 nm, BX/YCDs/SiO2 samples fixed at different cross-sectional positions of living poplar trees exhibited the maximum fluorescence intensity excitation peak at 540 nm, showing a blue shift. The wood fluorescence intensity of the sample at T0 was the lowest, while the wood fluorescence intensity of the sample at the injection port was the highest. As it gradually moved away from the injection port, the fluorescence intensity of poplar trees at various positions gradually decreased.

Fig. 12 shows the FTIR spectra of untreated wood and different position on the cross section of poplar samples at T0 treated with BX/YCD/SiO2 after the injection treatment. The characteristic absorption peaks of cellulose were more obvious at 1425 cm−1, 1370 cm−1 and 895 cm−1, hemicellulose at 1738 cm−1 and lignin at 1600 cm−1 and 1510 cm−1(Kelkar et al., 2023). Compared with untreated wood, the basic morphology of FTIR images of poplar samples at different height positions treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 showed no significant changes. After the injection treatment with BX/YCDs/SiO2, the absorption peaks at 1738 cm−1, 1510 cm−1, 1425 cm−1, and 895 cm−1 of samples at different heights were significantly reduced. The characteristic absorption peaks at 1738 cm−1, 1510 cm−1, 1425 cm−1, and 895 cm−1 of poplar samples on injection port disappeared, while the intensity of the characteristic absorption peaks at 1738 cm−1, 1600 cm−1,1510 cm−1, 1425 cm−1, and 895 cm−1 of poplar samples on middle position and far from injection port were reduced, possibly due to the reaction between cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin with BX/YCDs/SiO2. Compared with untreated wood, the poplar samples from injection port position to far from injection port position exhibited significant CO stretching vibration characteristic peaks of BX/YCDs/SiO2 at 1630 cm−1, indicating that BX/YCDs/SiO2 rose to this position.

Fig. 13 and Table 1display the XPS spectra of untreated wood and poplar treated by injection with BX/YCDs/SiO2, as well as the calculated content of four types of C atoms based on the relative peak area. The binding state of carbon atoms in wood can be typically categorized into four types. C1 represents the C atom that is not connected with atoms except C and H, that is, the carbon chain skeleton. C1 is mainly derived from wood lignin and wood extracts (fatty acids, waxes and terpenoids, etc.). C2 refers to carbon atom bonded to a single non-carbonyl oxygen atom, specifically a hydroxyl group. This carbon atom primarily originates from cellulose and hemicellulose in wood. C3 defined as a carbon atom connected with one carbonyl oxygen atom or two non-carbonyl oxygen atoms, which can include carbonyl, or aldehyde, or amide groups or two hydroxyl groups. C3 mainly comes from hemicelluloses and lignin. C4 is a C atom that connects a carbonyl O atom and a non-carbonyl O atom, that is, carboxyl, ester group and other groups. C4 This carbon atom is primarily from hemicelluloses. The electron binding energy of these four carbons is 285 eV, 286.5 eV, 288 eV or 289Ev (Li, 2003), respectively. As shown in Table 1, after treatment with BX/YCDs/SiO2 solution, the C4 content of poplar wood at the T0 injection port, middle position, and far from the injection port was 3.3 %, 2.7 %, and 2.0 %, respectively. This indicated that the closer the BX/YCDs/SiO2 was to the injection port on the cross-section of poplar wood after injection treatment, the higher the C4 content in the wood. The main reason for the increase in C4 content was that YCDs in BX/YCDs/SiO2 contained a large number of carboxyl groups, which reacted with borax to form ester groups. The C2 content of untreated wood was 45.2 %, while poplar samples treated after injection treatment with BX/YCDs/SiO2 solution, the C2 content of poplar at T0 injection port, middle position, and far from injection port was 30.5 %, 37.7 %, and 41.9 %, respectively. This indicated that, after the standing tree preservation treatment, the C2 content of poplar decreased closer to the injection port on the cross-section, possibly due to the reaction between BX/YCDs/SiO2 and some hydroxyl groups of cellulose and groups in hemicellulose in the wood cell wall.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Decay resistance of poplar injected BX/YCDs/SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 14 illustrates that the mass loss rate in the untreated group exceeded 44 % following 12 weeks of fungi infection testing. The decay resistance grade was assessed based on established standard, revealing the untreated wood samples were classified as the grade of no decay resistance. In contrast, most wood samples were categorized between strong and general decay resistance grades, demonstrating notable resistance to decay. As the height of poplar trees increased, the mass loss rate gradually increased and the corrosion resistance decreased. The mass loss rate of wood treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 solution increased from 9.4 % at T0 to 25.6 % at T12. This may be due to the formation of nano-SiO2 particles from tetraethyl orthosilicate, which reduced the smoothness of sap transport.

The leaching rate of wood preservatives BX/YCDs/SiO2 and BA/BX treated with injection treatment are respectively shown in Fig. 15. As time progressed, the leaching rate of wood preservatives showed an initial increase followed by a decrease. Between 0 and 24 h, the leaching rate of preservative solution increased sharply, resulting in a significant loss of the preservative. Between 24 and 48 h, there was still a significant leaching rate of preservative solutions, and between 24 and 48 h, the leaching rate of preservative solution decreased significantly. After 96 h, the leaching rate of preservative solution basically stopped. At 24 h, the leaching rate of each group of samples reached its maximum value, and the leaching rate of the wood after the injection treatment with the BX/YCDs/SiO2 solution was 42.7 %, decreased significantly compared with the leaching rate of the wood after the immersion treatment with BA/BX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of poplar injected BX/YCDs/SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 16 presents the mechanical properties of the wood following the injection treatment with BX/YCDs/SiO2 solutions. As shown in Fig. 16, the untreated wood had a flexural strength of 43 MPa and a modulus of elasticity (MOE) of 5614 MPa. In contrast, the wood samples treated with the BA/BX solution after injection treatment exhibited a flexural strength of 46 MPa and an MOE of 5246 MPa. The flexural strength of the wood treated with BA/BX following immersion treatment was similar with that of the untreated wood. The flexural strength and MOE of the poplar specimens treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 solution following injection treatment were 53 MPa and 5154 MPa, respectively. The flexural strength treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 after injection treatment increased by 23 % compared with untreated poplar wood, due to the energy accumulation on the treated wood against damage under flexural loads (Yona et al., 2021). The flexural MOE treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 after injection treatment decreased by 8.2 % compared with untreated poplar wood, which was similar to Wo ́zniak’s result (Woźniak et al., 2021).

The untreated wood exhibited a compressive strength of 19 MPa and an MOE of 5110 MPa. The compression strength, and compression MOE of the wood treated with BA/BX following immersion treatment were 21 MPa and 4864 MPa, respectively, not much different from the untreated poplar wood. The compression strength, and compression MOE of the wood samples treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 solution following injection treatment were 27 MPa and 5039 MPa, respectively. The compression strength treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 after injection treatment increased by 42 % compared with untreated poplar wood.

This research prepared yellow, fluorescent carbon dots using ortho-phenylenediamine as the carbon source. The yellow, fluorescent wood preservative BX/YCDs/SiO2 was prepared by combining YCDs with the wood preservative borax and encapsulating them with nano SiO2. The morphology and fluorescence performance of fluorescent preservatives were analyzed using various testing methods, along with their upward movement to different heights and cross-sectional positions of poplar trees. The following conclusions were drawn:

After treatment with BX/YCDs/SiO2 solution, as the height of the trees increased, poplar slices at different height positions showed different fluorescent colors under a fluorescence microscope. The fluorescence spectrum showed that, as the height of the tree increased, the fluorescence intensity at different height positions of the poplar tree gradually decreased after injection treatment. The SEM-EDS analysis results indicated that, as the height position of poplar trees increased, the content of boron element gradually decreased.

By studying and treating poplar wood with BX/YCDs/SiO2 solution at different positions from the injection port on the cross-section at T0, it was found that, as the distance from the injection port gradually increased, the fluorescence microscopy of poplar wood at different cross-sectional positions showed varying fluorescence colors. Regarding lateral penetration, the farther the distance from the injection port, the weaker the fluorescence intensity became. XPS scanning analysis was performed on the cell wall of treated wood. The increase in C4 content at different cross-sectional positions of poplar tree T0 after treatment with the BX/YCDs/SiO2 solution was attributed to the presence of a large number of ester groups in BX/YCDs/SiO2. Fluorescent preservatives were also distributed on the cell walls of the poplar trees.

The injection method for applying boron preservatives labeled with fluorescent carbon dots in standing trees has great potential for practical application in forestry management. The possibility of monitoring preservative distribution in trees enables the optimization of preservative volume and injection location, leading to a significant reduction in preservative chemical waste and environmental impact. The real-time visualization of fluorescent labeling allows the operator to visually determine the ideal preservative penetration depth during the treatment process and facilitates the pursuit of more even preservative application on the tree xylem.

The decay resistance of the wood injected using BX/YCDs/SiO2 significantly improved, and the leaching rate of the wood injected using BX/YCDs/SiO2 decreased. Compared to the wood treated by immersion with BA/BX, the flexural strength and compression strength of the poplar wood treated with BX/YCDs/SiO2 through injection treatment increased significantly.

Wen Liang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Su Hang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation. Sun Shijing: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Cai Liping: Writing – review & editing. Wang Xuan: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation, Conceptualization. Zhang Yaoli: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province （Youth Program）(BK20210610) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31670558).
